career.notes: 59 years as senior pastor of Eliot Congregational Church, Roxbury.

career.organization: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
career.notes: ABCFM deputation with Rev. Rufus Anderson to resolve educational policy at India and Sri Lanka missions.

education.school_name: Theological Seminary at East Windsor Hill, CT
education.notes: Studied theology at East Windsor Hill CT seminary.
Studied in Germany at University of Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor of Roxbury's Eliot Church for fifty-nine years
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Augustus Thompson began his pastorate at Eliot Congregational Church, Roxbury, Massachusetts in 1842 and remained senior pastor until his death on September 26, 1901 — fifty-nine unbroken years. In that time he published more than fifteen devotional books, outlived two wives and three children, and became one of the most prolific Congregationalist authors of the mid-Victorian era.

narrative_blocks.header: Led ABCFM deputation to India in 1854
narrative_blocks.body: In 1854-5, Thompson accompanied Rev. Dr. Rufus Anderson as the ABCFM's deputation to its missions in India and Sri Lanka — visiting stations to resolve disputes over educational policy at a time when British colonial grants were pressuring missionaries toward English-language instruction. The same visit brought him to the Madura mission in South India where William Banfield Capron — husband of Hooker descendant Sarah Brown Hooker Capron — was then serving.

Augustus Thompson began his pastorate at Eliot Congregational Church, Roxbury, Massachusetts in 1842 and remained senior pastor until his death on September 26, 1901 — fifty-nine unbroken years. In that time he published more than fifteen devotional books, outlived two wives and three children, and became one of the most prolific Congregationalist authors of the mid-Victorian era.

cross_connections.display_label: Husband Thompson's deputation visited Capron's Madura mission

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband outlived her by forty-four years
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Elizabeth Strong Thompson died May 11, 1857 at forty-eight years old in Somerville, Massachusetts — forty-four years before her husband Rev. Augustus Thompson, who lived to eighty-nine and served as senior pastor of Eliot Congregational Church in Roxbury for fifty-nine unbroken years. She saw five children born; he outlived three of them.

career.role: Assistant Paymaster → Pay Director → Rear Admiral
career.notes: Appointed Assistant Paymaster Oct 1863. Paymaster 1878. Pay Inspector July 1898. Pay Director Sept 1901. Retired Dec 26, 1903 as Rear Admiral. 40+ years service.

military_service.notes: Served on USS Massachusetts to close of Spanish-American War.

institutions.institution_blurb: Williams College class of 1862. Civil War service followed graduation.

narrative_blocks.header: Retired Rear Admiral from the U.S. Navy
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Strong Thompson served in the Civil War with the 45th Massachusetts Regiment, then spent forty years in the United States Navy's Pay Corps — rising from Assistant Paymaster in 1863 to Pay Director in 1901, retiring December 26, 1903 as a Rear Admiral. His official accounts across four decades are recorded as among the most complete and accurate ever received by the Naval Department.

narrative_blocks.header: Served on the Polaris expedition to the Arctic
narrative_blocks.body: Thompson sailed on the Polaris Search Expedition to the Arctic and later brought survivors of the Virginius massacre from Santiago de Cuba to New York aboard a Navy vessel. He also served on the battleship Massachusetts to the close of the Spanish-American War.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of a minister; grandson of the Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Strong Thompson was the son of Rev. Augustus Thompson, fifty-nine-year pastor of Eliot Congregational Church in Roxbury, and the grandson of Governor Caleb Strong of Massachusetts — one of the most prominent Federalist politicians of the early republic. The Rear Admiral thus carried both pastoral and gubernatorial lineage.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Springfield, Hampden County MA per FGA.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah Hooker; dead before her first birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Thompson was born February 21, 1846 and died February 10, 1847 — eleven days before turning one year old. She was the third generation to carry the name Sarah Hooker Strong.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Sarah Hooker — third generation to carry this name after H00525 and H02675.

narrative_blocks.body: Julia Augusta Thompson was born and died on April 22, 1848 — the second child of Sarah Elizabeth Strong and Rev. Augustus Thompson to die before adulthood. Her mother Sarah Elizabeth Strong lived only nine more years, dying in 1857.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned at Concord, New Hampshire at thirteen
narrative_blocks.body: Augustus Charles Thompson was born July 24, 1849 and drowned at Concord, New Hampshire on August 21, 1862 — thirteen years old. His mother Sarah Elizabeth Strong had died in 1857; his father Rev. Thompson remarried. The Hooker genealogy records his drowning with stark brevity.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST041.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.
